text: The surgical team demonstrates the placement of five ports on the patient's abdomen, which are used for access during the procedure.
An additional liver retractor is visible in the surgical field, positioned to provide exposure and stabilization of the liver during the procedure.
The robotic system's first arm is equipped with a fenestrated bipolar grasper, visible in the surgical field.
The laparoscopic robotic camera is positioned as the second arm in the surgical setup, providing a visual perspective of the operative field.
The third robotic arm is equipped with interchangeable instruments, including an electrocautery hook, a vessel sealer, or a robotic stapler.
The robotic system's fourth arm is equipped with caudate forceps, visible alongside the other robotic arms and the liver retractor in the surgical field.
The surgeon performs an intraoperative esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD) to visually confirm the placement of the gastrotomy site.
The tumor is visible within the gastric lumen, observed through the laparoscopic robotic camera.
The surgeon uses hook electrocautery to create the gastrotomy site, with precise incisions visible on the gastric tissue.
The laparoscopic camera is inserted into the gastrotomy site, providing a view of the intragastric tumor and confirming the correct placement of the gastrotomy.
The surgeon enlarges the gastrotomy site to facilitate the elevation and stapled resection of the intragastric tumor.
The surgeon continues to enlarge the gastrotomy site using surgical instruments, facilitating the mobilization of the gastric GIST visible within the gastric lumen.
The surgeon elevates the intragastric tumor, creating space for the surgical stapler to pass underneath the tumor's stalk.
The surgeon closes the gastrotomy site using a horizontal running V-lock suture, followed by a second imbricated layer with a vertical running V-lock suture.
Following the closure of the gastrotomy site, the surgeon performs an intraoperative esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD) to visually inspect the gastric interior.
The surgeon uses an intraoperative endoscope to examine the suture layers from inside the stomach, ensuring proper closure and integrity of the gastrotomy site.
The surgeon performs a negative leak test, visually confirming the integrity of the closed gastrotomy site.
The excised posterior wall gastric gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST) is displayed, showing the area where the tumor was removed.
